title: Ramesh Kumar Sharma v. Hindustan Agro Industries Ltd. & Anr.
keyArguments: The termination of the petitioner was effected without following the principles of natural justice as no show cause notice was issued prior to dismissal
The respondent company failed to conduct a proper domestic inquiry as mandated under the Standing Orders certified under the Industrial Employment (Standing Orders) Act, 1946
The alleged misconduct was not proven beyond reasonable doubt and the findings of the Inquiry Officer were perverse and unsupported by evidence on record
The petitioner had an unblemished service record of 14 years and the punishment of dismissal was disproportionate to the alleged misconduct
The termination violated Section 25-F of the Industrial Disputes Act, 1947 as retrenchment compensation was neither offered nor paid
judgmentSummary: The Delhi High Court allowed the writ petition filed by the petitioner-employee challenging his dismissal from service. The Court held that the domestic inquiry conducted by the respondent company was vitiated by procedural irregularities and violation of natural justice. The termination was set aside and the petitioner was directed to be reinstated with 50% back wages.
judgmentText:
IN THE HIGH COURT OF DELHI AT NEW DELHI
W.P.(C) No. 4471 of 2022

RAMESH KUMAR SHARMA ... PETITIONER
VERSUS
HINDUSTAN AGRO INDUSTRIES LTD. & ANR. ... RESPONDENTS

Date of Decision: 14th March, 2023

CORAM: HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SANJAY KUMAR MISHRA

JUDGMENT

1. The present writ petition under Article 226 of the Constitution of India has been filed by the petitioner challenging the order of dismissal dated 03.09.2021 passed by Respondent No.1 and the consequential order rejecting his appeal dated 17.12.2021.

2. Brief facts as necessary for adjudication are that the petitioner was employed as a Senior Technician with Respondent No.1 company since the year 2007 and had completed approximately 14 years of continuous service at the time of his dismissal. A charge sheet was issued against him on 15.06.2021 alleging unauthorized absence and misappropriation of company materials.

3. The petitioner submits that the Inquiry Officer appointed by the management had a predetermined mind and the entire inquiry proceeding was conducted in gross violation of the principles of natural justice. Specifically, the petitioner was not given adequate opportunity to cross-examine the management witnesses and certain documents relied upon by the management were never furnished to him despite repeated written requests.

4. Learned counsel for the petitioner Mr. Anil Verma placed reliance upon the judgment of the Hon'ble Supreme Court in Workmen of Firestone Tyre and Rubber Co. of India (P) Ltd. v. Management (1973) 1 SCC 813 and contended that a domestic inquiry which does not afford adequate opportunity to the delinquent employee to cross-examine witnesses is fundamentally flawed and cannot sustain in law.

5. Per contra, learned counsel for the respondents Mr. Deepak Sinha argued that the inquiry was conducted in accordance with the certified Standing Orders and that the petitioner was given full opportunity to participate. It is further contended that the findings of the Inquiry Officer are based on cogent evidence and this Court should not interfere in the exercise of its writ jurisdiction.

6. This Court has carefully considered the rival submissions and perused the inquiry record placed on file.

7. A perusal of the inquiry proceedings reveals that on two occasions the petitioner's written requests for supply of documents were simply ignored by the Presenting Officer. The Inquiry Officer's report does not record any reason for such denial. This Court is of the considered view that denial of relevant documents to a delinquent employee strikes at the root of fair inquiry and amounts to violation of natural justice as held in Union of India v. T.R. Varma AIR 1957 SC 882.

8. Furthermore, the charge of misappropriation which formed the primary basis for the extreme punishment of dismissal rests solely on the statement of one management witness whose testimony was internally contradictory. No corroborative material evidence was produced. The conclusion of guilt drawn by the Inquiry Officer is therefore perverse.

9. This Court also notes with concern that the petitioner had an unblemished service record spanning 14 years with no prior disciplinary proceeding. The punishment of dismissal in such circumstances is shockingly disproportionate and violates the settled principle of proportionality in service jurisprudence.

10. In view of the foregoing, the impugned order of dismissal dated 03.09.2021 and the appellate order dated 17.12.2021 are hereby quashed and set aside. The petitioner is directed to be reinstated in service with continuity of service from the date of dismissal. Given that the petitioner remained out of employment for approximately 18 months before securing alternative work, he shall be entitled to 50% back wages for the said period.

11. The respondent company shall comply with this order within eight weeks from the date of its receipt.

12. The writ petition is accordingly allowed with costs of Rs.25,000/- to be paid by Respondent No.1 to the petitioner.

(SANJAY KUMAR MISHRA)
JUDGE
MARCH 14, 2023
